1: Ephraim’s complaint repeats the pattern of 8:1-3 (their grievance against Gideon): inter-tribal jealousy over military honor. ṣāpônāh (to the north) indicates they crossed northward toward Gilead. The threat to burn Jephthah’s house escalates the confrontation from 8:1-3, where Gideon pacified Ephraim with diplomacy; Jephthah will not.
2: Jephthah’s rebuttal is terse and factual: ʾîš rîḇ hāyîtî waʿammî ûḇənê ʿammôn məʾōd — “I and my people were in great conflict with the Ammonites.” His claim that he called Ephraim and they did not come is unverified by the narrative of ch11 — but the narrator neither confirms nor denies it, leaving Jephthah’s assertion uncontested.
3: wāʾāśîmāh napšî bəḵappî — “I put my life in my own hands.” This idiom (lit. “placed my soul in my palm”) expresses risking one’s life; nāpāš (H5315) as the seat of life, kap (H3709) as the open palm. Compare 1 Sam 19:5 (Jonathan uses the same idiom for David). The phrase underlines that Jephthah acted without Ephraimite support and won anyway.
4: ʾattā pəlîṭê ʾeprayim ʾattā giləʿād — “You are fugitives of Ephraim, O Gilead.” This tribal insult — that Gileadites were Ephraimite refugees — was the provocation for the battle. The Transjordanian Israelites were viewed by Cisjordanian tribes as marginal; Ephraim’s slur triggers a civil war. wayyakke giləʿād ʾet-ʾeprayim — “Gilead struck Ephraim” — civil war, Israel killing Israel.
5: The Gileadites secured the Jordan fords (maʿabrôt hayyardēn) — strategic chokepoints across which Ephraimite survivors had to pass. The fords are the same terrain used in Gideon’s pursuit of Midian (7:24-25); controlling them means controlling who escapes.
6: ʾĕmār-nāʾ šibbōlet — “Say now Shibboleth.” šibbōlet (H7641) means either “ear of grain” or “flowing stream” — the phonological content mattered less than the dialectal distinction. Ephraimite dialect lacked the /ʃ/ phoneme, producing sibbōlet instead; the Gileadite ear detected the substitution. The result: 42,000 Ephraimite dead at the fords. This is the Hebrew Bible’s most explicit account of a dialectal accent being weaponized.
7: Jephthah’s tenure: six years. The formulaic close (wayyišpōṭ + years + death + burial) reduces one of Judges’ most complex figures to a brief summary. Burial “in one of the cities of Gilead” — the vagueness contrasts with the precision of minor-judge burial notices in vv.8-15, perhaps a textual lacuna.
8: Ibzan of Bethlehem — probably Bethlehem in Zebulun (Josh 19:15) rather than the Judean Bethlehem, given the Zebulunite context. šāpaṭ (H8199, judged) is the only term applied; no Spirit, no military action, no divine evaluation.
9: Thirty sons and thirty daughters: the large family is a status indicator in the ancient world, evidence of prosperity and influence. The exogamous marriage pattern — daughters sent out, daughters brought in from outside the clan — reflects political alliance-building through kinship ties. Ibzan commanded the resources to arrange sixty such alliances.
10: Burial at Bethlehem — the formulaic close for the minor judge cycle. The brevity of vv.8-10 mirrors the brevity of Ibzan’s recorded activity; these are administrative rather than narrative entries.
11: Elon the Zebulunite — ten-year tenure. No military action, no divine speech, no narrative. The sequence of minor judges (Ibzan, Elon, Abdon) creates a pattern of social stability between the turbulent stories of major judges; their formulaic notices mark passages of time in which Israel neither revolted nor was oppressed.
12: Buried at Aijalon in Zebulun — distinguished from Aijalon in Dan (Josh 19:42). The tribal identification haZzəḇûlônî frames the territory of his authority; burial location anchors the judge to his clan territory.
13: Abdon son of Hillel the Pirathonite — Pirathon is in the hill country of Ephraim (v15). Abdon’s Ephraimite origin immediately follows the Ephraimite civil war narrative of 12:1-7, creating an ironic reversal: the tribe that was nearly annihilated now supplies a judge.
14: Forty sons and thirty grandsons riding on seventy donkeys — the highest-status marker among the minor judges (Jair had thirty sons on thirty donkeys, 10:4; Abdon has seventy mounts across two generations). Riding a donkey signals aristocratic status in the ancient Near East (Num 22, 1 Sam 25:20); the number of mounts signals the breadth of his household’s honor.
15: Buried at Pirathon in Ephraim, in the Amalekite hill country — bəhar hāʿămālêqî notes that this region was once occupied by Amalek, a reminder that the land Israel inhabits came at a cost. The minor judge series closes here; Samson’s annunciation follows immediately as the next era begins.
